title: Witnessing entanglement between photon and matter due to graviton exchange
authors: Arijit Dutta, Marko Toroš, Sougato Bose, Anupam Mazumdar
abstract: The paper presents a scheme to detect entanglement arising from the quantum nature of gravity between a spin qubit and photons, using Stokes parameters. One of the crucial tests of the general theory of relativity is the bending of light due to the curvature. Recently, a quantum counterpart of this experiment to test the quantum nature of the gravitational interaction has been proposed, in which the spin-2, massless graviton yields entanglement between matter and a photon sector. Hence, it provides one of the most crucial experimental signatures for testing the quantum nature of gravity in a lab, since only spin-2-induced entanglement can yield the correct deflection of light due to matter. Here, we propose a positive partial-transpose (PPT) witness criterion for witnessing such an entanglement. We scan the entangled states in this context by studying the overlap of the final state, which is proportional to the entanglement phase. We exploit the Stokes observables to measure the photon state and the spins in the matter sector, thereby constructing a witness for the quantum nature of gravity in this setup. To quantify this entanglement, we will couple the photon to a local oscillator, whose phase need to be controlled to probe the orthogonal components of the macroscopic interference in the laser beam. We have shown that for a non-maximally entangled state mediated by the quantum nature of gravity, the witness attains a maximal negativity of $-0.052$. Our findings indicate that this witness effectively detects entanglement within the range $0.71 \leq |γ| < 1$, where $γ$ is the overlap between the two coherent states of the photon, providing a clear signature of quantum correlations.
summary: This paper proposes a method to witness entanglement between a photon and a massive particle mediated by the exchange of virtual gravitons. By using a high-intensity laser and a massive particle in spatial superposition, the authors derive an entanglement witness based on Stokes parameters. The study quantifies the effectiveness of this witness and determines the limits of its robustness against isotropic noise. This provides a potential experimental pathway to test the quantum nature of gravity in a laboratory setting.
main_problem: Developing an experimental scheme and an entanglement witness to detect quantum correlations between a photon and a massive particle induced by graviton exchange.
main_result: The authors propose a PPT-based witness using Stokes parameters that can effectively detect entanglement in the regime 0.71 <= |gamma| < 1, with a maximum negativity of -0.052.
method: The study employs the Peres-Horodecki (PPT) criterion to derive an observable witness, mapping effective Pauli operators to physical Stokes operators and using a local oscillator for phase-sensitive measurement.
model_or_system: A bipartite system consisting of a massive particle (spin qubit) in a spatial superposition and an optical field in a strong coherent state, interacting via a perturbative framework of massless spin-2 graviton exchange.
key_observables: Stokes parameters (S0, S1, S2, S3), photon-number difference, and the overlap parameter |gamma|.
important_parameters: Mass m (0.1 to 10 kg), spatial separation (up to 100 um), photon intensity (|alpha|^2 = 10^13), and interaction time (1 s).
main_assumptions: The interaction is modeled via a perturbative quantum field theoretic framework; the system is subject to isotropic noise; the particle is treated as an effective qubit in a two-dimensional subspace.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the interaction schematic; Table I displays the overlap |gamma| as a function of mass and separation; Figure 2 plots overlap vs. spatial separation; Figure 3 shows the witness value vs. |gamma|; Figure 4 illustrates the relationship between |gamma| and the critical noise threshold.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of detecting gravity-induced entanglement, describes the physical model and interaction Hamiltonian, derives the entanglement witness using the PPT criterion, proposes an experimental measurement strategy using Stokes parameters and a local oscillator, and concludes with a noise robustness analysis.
why_interesting: It bridges quantum optics and quantum gravity by proposing a concrete, measurable signature (entanglement witness) for the spin-2 nature of gravitons using macroscopic optical observables.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 2. Overlap |γ| as a function of spatial separation xl−xr for dif- ferent masses (see Eq. (15), and Eq. (17)). Parameters: |α| = 1013, τ = 1 s, r0 = 0.25 m, λ = 1 µm., we see how |γ| scales with the superposition size. Note that |γ| = 1 shows no entanglement; hence, for small values of |γ|, it is required to witness the entanglement, which prefers heavier masses to be kept in a large spatial superposi- tion.

FIG. 3. Entanglement witness ⟨ΠTM −⟩meas as a function of the coherent-state overlap |γ|. The witness exhibits a non-monotonic de- pendence, attaining maximal negativity around |γ| ≈0.92, reflecting a trade-off between distinguishability and measurement sensitivity.

FIG. 4. Plot of |γ| vs vcritical, (see Eq. (E1)) showing the depen- dence of the overlap parameter |γ| on the noise parameter v. For any pure state of the mass-photon combined system (23) (or any bipartite system) mixed with the isotropic noise, the minimum critical noise parameter vcritical is 1
